entry.title: Re: 4.3 Web Intelligence Data Lineage Challenge
entry.content:
Thanks for the response Christian.

I would challenge the assumption that the universe objects from 2 different queries albeit from the same universe are the same and should be renamed in this manner.

It effectively assumes that you are joining on all the dimensions from both queries, but what if you are only joining on one dimension in the query because you want compare the values in the other dimensions (joining in data mode is some ways similar to merging dimensions, but obviously more powerful)

So 2 queries with the same dimensions X,Y and you choose to join only on X because you want to compare the values being stored in dimension Y in both queries.

I only started to think about renaming the objects in the original queries because you cannot identify the lineage of the objects from their original queries. I ended up renaming the objects in the cube, but it's a tricky exercise. Tooltip is a start, but perhaps colorising by data source would be clearer

 

thanks again for engaging

 

James

entry.title: Re: CALM & MS Teams Integration changes due to MS O365 co...
entry.content:
Hi,

On the contrary, the configuration in your second image ("USING WORKFLOWS") is actually the same whether you were previously using MS Teams Incoming Webhooks or are now using MS Teams Workflows. 

The MS Teams documentation Create an Incoming Webhook illustrates that the Incoming Webhooks are created per-channel, not per Team or per Installation. I can't find any evidence that MS Teams Incoming Webhooks can be shared across different Teams or Channels. So this means that you already needed to create individual BTP destinations for each MS Teams Channel that was using an MS Teams Incoming Webhook. 

Here is a graphic that represents the hierarchy and structure a bit clearer. It incorporates both MS Teams Incoming Webhooks and MS Teams Workflows.

SAP Cloud ALM already supports the newer MS Teams Workflows. They have to be maintained in much the same manner as the older MS Teams Incoming Webhooks. In other words, each MS Teams Workflow needs its own BTP destination, the same way that each MS Teams Incoming Webhook needed its own BTP destination. Then a "webhook" must be created in the External API Management "Webhooks management" screen. 

The Devs have no plans to add a specific "Workflows Management" screen in the SAP Cloud ALM External API Management app. The name "Webhooks Management" in the External API Management app won't change either. This is because "webhook" in the SAP Cloud ALM context describes a general concept (a connection between an "External API Management" subscription and a BTP destination for an external API), and not a specific technology (MS Teams Incoming Webhooks). 

I hope that this clarifies this point for you.

Thanks and regards, 
John

entry.title: Encryption using a public key with function SSF_KRN_ENVELOPE
entry.content:
Hi,

We need to encrypt a KEY, like 

<KEY>290B0B57A7C7AD3D9DF170511C1C9D209DAB1C7F7675D370</KEY>

For this we need to use a public key using algorithm PKCS v1.2., to get a key of 512 hexadecimal characters.

We are trying the following:

* We have defined a new SSF application.

* We have used transaction STRUST to upload the certificate that contains the public key to be used for the encryption.

* We have configured the SSF parameters for this new application:

           HASHALG ==> SHA512

           ENCRALG ==> TRIPLE-DES

* We use functions:

     ==> SSF_GET_PARAMETER(Read certificates included in SSFA)

     ==> SSFC_GET_CERTIFICATELIS (to get  the information of each certificate)

     ==> SSFC_PARSE_CERTIFICATE 

     ==> SSF_KRN_ENVELOPE (to encrypt), here we complete:

ostr_input_data with the string we want to encrypt:<KEY>290B0B57A7C7AD3D9DF170511C1C9D209DAB1C7F7675D370</KEY> 

we are not sure wich value we need to populte field  ostr_input_data_l .Based on method EXTERNAL_ENCYPTING of class CL_PCA_SECURITY, we are applying the same logic:

* preparations
    lv_length         = strlen( iv_card_number ).
* Unicode or Non-unicode systems
    lv_length         = lv_length * lv_bytes.

So in our case we are getting: ostr_input_data_l = 118

The function SSF_KRN_ENVELOPE returns ostr_enveloped_data, we have try to apply different conversions to this encrypted data but we get 510 characters instead of 512, so we not sure if we are missing something.

entry.title: Digital Manufacturing - Production Process Monitoring with SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Hi all,
in this blog, I would like to share a sample how to setup and use Job & Automation Monitoring in SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management (SAP Cloud ALM) to monitor Digital Manufacturing Production Processes centrally.
This capability will be available with the Digital Manufacturing 2411 release.

If you are not yet aware about SAP Cloud ALM, I encourage you to start with the learning journey for Introducing SAP Cloud ALM or to take a look on the overview page.

Introduction

SAP Cloud ALM Job and Automation Monitoring provides a central job monitoring solution for all SAP products to complement local job scheduling. It gives you transparency about the status of the different automation processes (called “jobs” for simplification) regarding execution status, application status, start delay, and run time. 

With the SAP Digital Manufacturing 2411 release, we enabled the Job & Automation Monitoring functionality in SAP Cloud ALM to support the new job type SAP DM Production Process.

This allows you to monitor SAP Digital Manufacturing production processes in one main place, including the following SAP Cloud ALM capabilities:

  • Centrally monitor the health of job executions and detect anomalies during execution,
    including direct navigation to the local production process monitoring in SAP Digital Manufacturing.
  • Promptly alert both business and IT users about disruptions and exceptions.
    You can configure events in SAP Cloud ALM to inform stakeholders who need to react to exceptions.
  • Use the analysis view to gain insights on historical execution information.
    You can use the analysis view to identify the jobs with the longest response times and highest number of exceptions. A trend icon indicates if the failure rate or response time is up or down in the selected time frame, taking the midpoint as the comparison point.

Note: Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM shouldn’t replace the local SAP Digital Manufacturing Monitor Production Processes app but rather complement it to support monitoring and collaboration between different stakeholders.

Prerequisites

  • You’ve SAP CLOUD ALM provisioned.
    If you don’t have an SAP Cloud ALM tenant yet, check out the SAP Cloud ALM support page, where you can find all the required information.
    Note: SAP Cloud ALM is part of SAP Enterprise Support. The usage rights are included in the SAP Cloud Service subscription and in SAP Enterprise Support. Details on usage rights please check out Usage Rights.
  • You’ve completed the user authorization setup.
  • The SAP Digital Manufacturing Service is available in SAP Cloud ALM Landscape Management. This should happen automatically.
    Cloud services that you subscribe to are automatically imported and updated from SAP's Cloud Landscape Directory. The initial import contains all cloud services that belong to the same customer number as SAP Cloud ALM. You can maintain a Corporate Group (CCC) S-User to also import cloud services that belong to subsidiaries of the same corporate group. For more information, see Landscape Setup.  

SAP Cloud ALM Setup & Configuration

Select Your Scope for Job & Automation Monitoring

To see your SAP Digital Manufacturing service in SAP Cloud ALM, you first need to make sure you’ve set the scope accordingly. Open your SAP Cloud ALM application and navigate to Operations -> Job & Automation Monitoring
choose Scope.

In the Select a Scope popup, search for Service Type Digital Manufacturing, which will bring up your tenants.

Configuration

For general configuration of SAP Cloud ALM Job & Automation Monitoring, see  https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/jm-configuration

Choose the configuration wheel to open the configuration panel.

Managed Components

Switch on Data Collection for Job & Automation Monitoring

You can directly switch on the data collection using the toggle switch, or you can choose the name, and a detailed configuration popup will be displayed. Once you switch on the data collection for the system all new production process executions will be pushed to SAP Cloud ALM and visible in the central monitor.

Note: Only production process instances that were running at the point in time when you enabled data collection or are started later will be transferred. No historical data will be pushed to SAP Cloud ALM.
Production Process will send instances to SAP Cloud ALM every 10 mins with the data generated in the last 10 mins.

If you select a system, you can also add events to enable alerting. For more information, see in the Alerting chapter. 
For more information on managed components, application settings, and monitoring rating rules, see  https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/jm-configuration.

Note: High run times are only considered as yellow or red if the run time is longer than 1 minute. For SAP Digital Manufacturing processes, this setting might be too long.

Capabilities

Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM

Overview – The overview provides a summary status of production process executions. It shows the status of the latest execution of every job with regards to the technical application exceptions or the run time. It also displays the number of unconfirmed alerts and status events. 

Choosing a card takes you to the monitoring view, which shows the list of production processes grouped by the production process design with their latest execution status, application status, start delay, and runtime. In this view, we still see an aggregation of the instances processed in the selected time frame. 

Note: In the current version, we don’t differentiate between technical exceptions and application exceptions. The mapping between SAP Digital Manufacturing statuses and SAP Cloud ALM statuses is as follows:

DM StatusCALM Execution StatusCALM Application Status
RunningGreyGrey
FailedRedGrey
Completed with ErrorGreenGrey
CompletedGreenGrey
TerminatedYellowGrey

You can filter for a specific production process design, for example ResourceStatusChange. 

By choosing the production process design name, you can further drill down to the list of executions of the selected production process design. In the example below, 216 calls of the production process ResourceStatusChange / 1.4 have been executed. 7 instances failed and one call shows an exceptional long runtime of app. 10 sec.

Additionally, choosing the information icon provides further details and enables direct navigation to the job within the managed component by selecting the Run ID.

Further drill-down on instance level  will also show you the different steps executed in the production process execution. 

Alerting

You can configure alerts using the configuration dialog. Open the configuration dialog by choosing the configuration wheel.

Choose the component for which you would like to set the configuration.

Choose the service name. Now you can create events.

Event Settings

In the context of SAP Digital Manufacturing, we support the following cases:

  • Critical Application / Execution Status
    Note: Check details on SAP Digital Manufacturing status mapping.
  • Critical Runtime

Critical delay isn’t applicable for type SAP DM Production Processes as of today. 

Raise Event Action
By setting the Raise Event Action, you can define how you would like to get informed.  "At new occurrence" means when there’s a change to the event rating (to red or yellow). As a consequence, no new notification is sent on consecutive failures of the same job.

If you need a new notification at every job execution failure, you can configure to get the event action triggered "At every occurrence".

As SAP Digital Manufacturing runs the PPD for multiple plants with different parameters, which can cause different behavior for the different production process executions, it’s recommended to set the Raise Event Action to “At every occurrence”.

Event Filters
Within the configuration, you can set Event Filters, which means you can specify alerts for specific automation jobs (production process designs) or for specific steps / executables (production process steps).

Limitation:
At the moment, we don’t pass information from SAP Digital Manufacturing to SAP Cloud ALM for the following parameters:

  • Step Executed by
  • Job / Automation Scheduled by

Therefore, no filter options are possible for those parameters for the SAP DM Production Processes type. Depending on your configuration, you’ll receive an information e.g. via an Alert or Email.

Alerts are raised per job and alert type and contain all failed executions of a specific job.
Note: The alert has the status closed as the last execution was successful.

Analysis 

The Analysis view allows users to pinpoint jobs with extended response times and highest number of exceptions. For any job it shows aggregated information on total and average run time, number of executions and number of failed executions and the resulting failure rate.
In the example below we can see that ResourceStatusChange / 1.4 has the highest number of failed instances, however the previous version 1.3 shows a long runtime.
By clicking on the arrow at the end of every line, we can drill down into various trend charts for the metrics. 

SAP Cloud ALM Analytics API

In case you would like to store and build your own dashboards please check out the SAP Cloud ALM Analytics API. They will enable you to build dashboards and reports aggregating all types of data managed by SAP Cloud ALM for operations.

For details check out: https://support.sap.com/en/alm/sap-cloud-alm/operations/expert-portal/calm-apis-for-operations.html

Conclusion

In this blog post, you learned how to enable and configure the Job & Automation Monitor to in Cloud ALM for Production Processes to incorporating them into your overall landscape monitoring for efficient operation of the end-to-end solution.

Do you like this post? Please let me know what you think in the comments section.  Any feedback is greatly appreciated. If you have any questions, please visit the SAP Community Q&A Area or comment down below.  And last but not least some additional links to useful information.

  • SAP Cloud ALM for Operations Expert Portal
    https://support.sap.com/en/alm/sap-cloud-alm/operations/expert-portal.html
  • Blog: Job and Automation Monitoring features - SAP Cloud ALM https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/job-and-automation-monitoring-features-sap-cloud-alm/ba-p/13643600
  • Blog: Digital Manufacturing - Integration Message Monitoring with SAP Cloud ALM
    https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blogs-by-sap/digital-manufacturing-integration-message-monitoring-with-sap-cloud-alm/ba-p/13751158

entry.title: Re: Uploading Attachments’ – Attach any File (PDF/DOC/JPG/XLS/TXT..) through the OData
entry.content:
It's critical to ensure Virus Scanning is active at the Gateway-level, in order to prevent malware uploads over ODATA.

There is no need, however, to code this yourself. The Gateway will take care of it. Check /IWFND/VIRUS_SCAN and activate it, before using this in your applications.

cheers, 

Joerg

entry.title: Tricentis Test Automation for SAP - Authorization Management in real Projects
entry.content:
Documentation and Implementation Guide

A comprehensive guide to implement role-based access control in enterprise test management

 

Table of Contents

  1. Introduction
  2. Initial Situation
  3. Requirements
  4. Technical Implementation
  5. Authorization Concept
  6. Workflows
  7. Best Practices
  8. Appendix
  9. Summary

1. Introduction

This document outlines the Authorization management concept for implementing Tricentis Tosca in a multi-departmental enterprise environment with diverse user roles. It serves as a comprehensive guide for both initial implementation and ongoing system maintenance.

The prerequisite for the implementing the authorization concept is that the user administration server is set up, see: Tricentis Test Automation Server for SAP – Server for User Administration
Alternatively, you can perform user administration standalone directly in SAP TTA.

2. Initial Situation

The organization is implementing a test management system using Tosca for its HR and Logistics departments. Each department has its own developers and testers. Additionally, external auditors require read access to all testing activities.

3. Requirements

3.1 Core Principles

  • Each user belongs to exactly one group
  • Groups can be structured hierarchically
  • Authorizations are inherited through the hierarchy
  • Strict separation between departments
  • Business units can be represented through folders
  • Folders inherit their Authorizations to subfolders

3.2 Role-Specific Requirements

  • Developers (per department)
    • Create and edit test cases
    • Read-only access to test executions

 

  • Testers (per department)
    • Read-only access to test cases
    • Full access for test execution

 

  •  Auditors
    • Read access to all areas
    • No editing access

 

4. Technical Implementation

4.1 Folder Structure

Both test case creation and test execution follow this schematic structure: 

 

 

You can see that there is one folder for HR and one for Logistics. Under this folder, the corresponding subareas for HR or logistics. The test cases or test executions are then attached to these subareas.

The folders inherit their authorizations to the subareas. It is sufficient to assign the relevant authorizations only to the area folders (HR or Logistic).

4.2 Hierarchical Group Structure

To implement the principle "Each user belongs to exactly one group," a specific group without users must be created for each function and main folder. These groups contain the corresponding functional groups with actual users.

 

Example of a group implementation for HR department in Tricentis Server for User Authorization:

 

5. Authorization  Concept 

This detailed authorization assignment ensures that each user group receives exactly the authorizations they need for their specific tasks while maintaining the security and integrity of the overall system.

5.1 Specific Groups per Folder Read-Only / All

The folder-specific read-only groups enable granular, read-only access to individual folder areas. These groups are ideal for stakeholders who only need to view specific subsections.

  • Assigned to respective folders
  • READ – ViewingGroupName
  • ALL - OwningGroupName

5.2 Developer Groups

The developer groups are specifically tailored to the needs of test case developers. They enable the creation and maintenance of test cases while maintaining control over execution results.

  • Inherits from: Respective All or Read Group
  • Additional access :
    • Full access in TestCases area
    • Read-only access to Execution area

5.3 Tester Groups

The tester groups are responsible for the practical execution of tests. Their Authorizations are configured to focus on test execution and result documentation.

  • Inherits from: Respective Base Group
  • Additional access:
    • Read-only access to TestCases area
    • Full access in Execution area

5.4 Auditor Group

The auditor group is specifically designed for external auditors who need comprehensive but controlled insight into all test activities.

  • Inherits from: Respective HR_READ and Logistics_READ Groups
  • Restrictions:
    • No editing access
    • No access to SharedResources

5.5 Example Implementation for HR Test Cases folder

The concrete implementation in Tosca could look as follows for the HR area:

 

6. Workflows

The following section describes the main operational workflows within Tricentis Tosca, detailing the interaction patterns between different user roles and the system. These workflows ensure a structured approach to test case management, execution, and auditing while maintaining proper segregation of duties.

6.1 Test Case Development

A systematic process for creating and managing test cases while maintaining clear separation between development and execution roles.

  1. Developer creates test case in test cases - The developer designs and implements test cases within their designated department folder, including test steps, test data, and expected results.
  2. Tester automatically sees test case - Through the Authorization system, testers gain immediate read access to newly created test cases in their department, enabling them to review and prepare for execution.
  3. Developer can view execution results - Developers maintain read-only access to test results, allowing them to analyse outcomes and refine test cases as needed.

6.2 Test Execution

A structured approach to test execution that ensures proper documentation and traceability of test results.

  1. Tester selects test case - The tester identifies and opens the appropriate test case from their department's test case library for execution.
  2. Executes test - The tester performs the test according to the defined steps, documenting actual results and any deviations from expected behavior.
  3. Saves results - Test results, including execution logs, screenshots, and any relevant attachments, are stored in the designated Execution folder.

6.3 Audit Process

A comprehensive review process that enables external auditors to evaluate testing activities across all departments.

  1. Auditor accesses all test areas with read authorization - Auditors gain read-only access to both HR and Logistics departments, including all test cases, execution results, and associated documentation.
  2. Views all test cases and results - Auditors can examine the complete testing lifecycle, from test case design to execution results, across all departments and modules.
  3. Can generate reports - Auditors have the ability to create standardized reports from the system, documenting test coverage, execution results, and compliance status.

7. Best Practices

The following best practices provide guidelines for maintaining a secure, efficient, and well-organized authorization management system in Tricentis Tosca. These practices ensure long-term sustainability and compliance while reducing administrative overhead and security risks.

7.1 Group Management

A systematic approach to organizing and maintaining user groups that ensures clarity and manageability.

  • Clear naming convention - Implement and maintain consistent group naming patterns (e.g., "Department_Role_Function") that clearly indicate the group's purpose and scope.
  • Hierarchy documentation - Maintain up-to-date documentation of the group hierarchy structure, including inheritance relationships and Authorization flows between groups.
  • Regular review - Conduct periodic reviews of the group structure to ensure it remains aligned with organizational needs and remove or consolidate unused groups.

7.2 Authorization Assignment

Guidelines for implementing the principle of least privilege while maintaining operational efficiency.

  • Minimal necessary access - Grant users only the Authorizations required for their specific role and responsibilities, avoiding over-privileged accounts that could pose security risks.
  • Utilization of inheritance - Leverage the hierarchical group structure to efficiently manage Authorizations through inheritance, reducing administrative overhead and potential configuration errors.
  • Avoidance of exceptions - Minimize individual Authorization exceptions by designing group structures that accommodate common use cases, making the system easier to maintain and audit.

7.3 Maintenance

Ongoing activities required to maintain the integrity and effectiveness of the Authorization system.

  • Regular reviews - Conduct scheduled reviews of Authorizations and access patterns to identify and address any deviations from intended use or security policies.
  • Change documentation - Maintain detailed records of all changes to group structures, Authorizations, and membership, including the rationale for changes and approvals.
  • Membership audits - Regularly verify group memberships against current organizational roles to ensure users are assigned to appropriate groups and remove outdated access.

These best practices help establish a robust framework for managing Authorizations in Tricentis Tosca while ensuring security, maintainability, and compliance with organizational policies. Regular application of these practices helps prevent Authorization sprawl, reduces security risks, and maintains system efficiency over time. The practices should be reviewed and updated periodically to reflect changes in organizational needs and emerging security requirements.

8. Appendix

8.1 Glossary

Term

Definition

Inheritance

Passing of access through the hierarchy

ViewingGroupName

Configuration for read Authorizations

OwningGroupName

Configuration for write Authorizations

8.2 Implementation Checklist

  •   Create group structure
  •   Set up folder structure
  •   Configure Authorizations
  •   Create test users
  •   Test access rights
  •   Finalize documentation
  •   Conduct training

9. Summary - Authorization Management in Tricentis Tosca

This comprehensive guide details the implementation of authorization management in Tricentis Test Automation for SAP, focusing specifically on a practical scenario involving HR and Logistics departments. The document establishes fundamental principles for user authorization, including single group membership and hierarchical structuring, while providing detailed technical implementation guidelines for folder structures and permission inheritance. The authorization concept introduces specific folder-based groups with varying access levels, covering the needs of developers, testers, and auditors, supported by practical examples using Tricentis Server for User Authorization. Three main operational workflows - test case development, test execution, and audit processes - are outlined with clear steps and responsibilities to ensure proper process separation. The guide concludes with best practices for group management, authorization assignment, and maintenance procedures, creating a balanced approach between security requirements and practical usability for both initial setup and ongoing system maintenance.

entry.title: Re: Automatic Start of Production Process
entry.content: If you want to start the process based on an integration, I would check the Automatic Triggers app (Business Rules tab). There are some events available when you can start a process, for example Order Created would be triggered when you send an order from ERP to DM. Another way could be to modify the integration artifact in Cloud Integration and trigger the process from there via API. But this would be much more complex and less standard.

entry.title: Procurement Monthly - October 2024
entry.content:
This is a blog by Gordon Donovan

Summary

Risk, cost resilience, Data, AI and digital. These are the main themes that emerge from this months reports. Procurement gravitating to much more of a risk based function. Organisations everywhere wanting to at least pilot AI use cases, mainly to concentrate more time on risk and complexity, and whilst inflation may be coming down, worries of recension, geopolitical uncertainty are still dominating agendas.

 

Procurement Leaders – Scope 3

This study of the PL membership looks at how procurement functions are measuring and managing the scope 3 emissions and some of the potential problems they are facing.

 We had suppliers that were switching from coal in some of their factories, but in a factory that doesn't make our product. Their overall emissions reduced greatly because of the switch, but it didn't help my product at all

The paper suggest 4 steps to help tackle the challenge.

  • Build the capability to progress data quality
  • Partner internally to incorporate data into the process
  • Segment suppliers and tailor engagement methods
  • Explore working with competitors to lighted the load

As you can see from the 4 steps, one of the biggest challenges on scope 3 is the accuracy (and relevance) of the data with the paper stating that 33% identified this as the main barrier for calculating scope 3 emissions.

The report also identifies that you cant impact Scope 3 without your suppliers (as its them who are creating the emission!

Currently, the top engagement method is emails with 87.5%, with training 75% and events and policy just over 50%. Only 5% of respondents said suppliers were not engaged which is a good outcome.

 

AQPC Procurement – Procurement Blue Print

This pretty comprehensive (74 page) book covers a range of benchmarks and findings from the various surveys that they carry out.

Some interesting stats total cost to perform procurement (this is P2P) is 2.80 per $1000 in revenue. Cycle times 1 day for issuing goods  or services PO. 90% of PO approved electronically,1473 PO processed per FTE

There are also some data points for supplier management, risk management and supply chain visibility that are worth while diving into as is the SAP Benchmarking report as well for comparison.

 

CIPS Outlook

One of the biggest impacts this year has been the risk of inflation, and of the amount of political events that could impact both inflation and potential recessions. The chief economist for Chartered Institute of Procurement & Supply (CIPS) reviews a quarterly pulse survey from (mainly) UK and Europe CPO’s.

Key takeaways are that headline risk is declining (slowly) from a very high point (4.53 to 4.02) in the last 6 months. Geopolitical factors continue to be the primary drivers of experience shortages in supply chains. With over 70% of experience shortages being attributed to this factor. Labour shortages continue to drive 43% of experience shortages in supply chains, which reflects high levels of employment in many economies.

Inflationary pressures in the global economy have abated. Inflation is moving into or close to central bank targets in Europe, the US and throughout the rest the world.  From the supply chain perspective it is unlikely that the disruption in the Red Sea is likely to be resolved any time soon. As a result, sea cargo will continue to transit around the Cape, increasing both transit times and cost. It is therefore not unsurprising that where procurement costs have increased by more than 10%, one-in-five of the drivers of these cost increases have been associated with logistics costs.

Given the levels of risk still existing in global supply chains, the key strategies that procurement managers and supply chain professionals are going to deploy to mitigate risk have not changed. Specifically, supply chain managers will continue to try and diversify the number of suppliers, extend contracts and hold more stocks. These three strategies have been the top ranked strategies in all three pulse studies conducted in 2024.

 

Oliver Wyman Procurement Study

In this survey, the authors sought detailed perspective on the most important issues for procurement professionals and the tactics they’re employing to tackle them effectively.

From a sourcing perspectives these priorities were unlocking supplier relationship value, mitigating inflation, and adjustment and refining of processes and strategies.  It wouldn’t be a survey unless it includes some aspect of AI!

In this study, the barriers for adoption were the lack of expertise, and integration with existing systems, followed by data quality and (interestingly) resistance to change from within procurement teams. Contract management, planning and renegotiation led the functionality of AI being implemented or considered.

 

Procurecon European CPO Study

Another survey this time from Procurecon, focusing on European CPOs was released this month.

The research indicates the CPO’s influence on C-suite decision-making has increased in 91% of organisations. Notably, no respondents reported a decrease. This follows the The Economist report of the last couple of years so its good to see this consistency.

Apparently AI is a thing! It features prominently in CPOs’ strategies moving forward. To free up resources and focus on ‘higher-level’ targets. The survey reveals 49% of respondents have already implemented AI solutions into select processes.

Echoing the CIPS report earlier, the report stated that Supply chain disruptions were cited as the most significant challenge by 26% of respondents, and inflation was the most challenging for 19%.

“I think automating simple tasks is the area that AI can bring the biggest value to procurement, so that you can focus resources on the priority tasks”

There is an interesting section around AI, both with a priorities and also a how to measure which I think is really interesting as procurement seeks to measure the value it brings beyond savings alone.

The areas for AI that  respondents are prioritising supplier risk management (54%), strategic sourcing & supplier selection (50%) and contract negotiation & management (47%) with measures including increased efficiency , better/faster decisions and improved supplier relationships leading the way. I was surprised to see cost savings low at 28% (as a benefit of AI) bearing in mind sourcing was so high as an area where it will be deployed however.

The demand for cost management persists (and in my opinion always will be) According to the research, spend analysis and category management (40%), supplier performance management (40%), and technology for automation and data analysis (38%) are the most effective methods deployed for managing costs within organisations.

 

EY Supply Chain Influence

This supply chain survey about supply chain influence was released this month from EY.

 

Modernizing the supply chain through technology and digitalization is one of the most pressing challenges for supply chain leaders in the past year. Leaders overall consider supply chain modernization through tech/digital a key priority today. There is however some disaconnects between C suite and leaders. There is a notable gap between the expectations of C-suite and supply chain executives regarding digital maturity within the organization and the timeline for their organizations’ supply chains to become mostly autonomous, with the C-suite more conservative and skeptical compared with supply chain executives:

  • The C-suite (26%) say that their organizations’ digital connectivity with suppliers is limited to email and sharing spreadsheets (vs. 16% of supply chain executives).
  • The C-suite (25%) are less likely to believe their organizations’ supply chains will be mostly autonomous by 2030 (vs. 39% of supply chain executives).

Risk management and revenue growth, supply chain resilience and agility have become paramount. Resiliency is firmly established as a top supply chain objective, with 87% of supply chain leaders overall saying their organizations have made significant investments to improve it. Yet, 24% of supply chain leaders report that their organization is not very or at all prepared for a pandemic or widespread health crisis, and 19% are similarly unprepared for supply shortages, signalling the need for further enhancement of supply chain robustness.

 

Supplier.IO State of Supplier Diversity

The 7th iteration of this report was released this month and the awareness and reporting to the exec level is increasing with 56% now saying that these results make it to the board (2x increase from 2021).

Key drivers are also changing with notably one of the new ones this year being the ability to win new business for their organisations (48%), which really does help procurement with the business value and contribution to growth targets that many now have and its notable that customer requirements as a key driver has increased as has compliance.

Total spend is still the standard metric  with impact, (both for your business and an economic impact of the program being key measurables as well.

Tracking suppliers leans much more toward 3rd party accreditation rather than self-certified being included in the data.

 

 

Gartner – Supply Chain Technology Trends

This article is based on a a few studies that Gartner has carried out over recent periods. 

The top trends this year have two broad themes: “control and protect” and “humans and machines.” These trends are interconnected, and their importance differs not only by organizational maturity but also by industry, business needs and previously devised strategic plans. Understanding the business strategy and deciding how procurement will support it are critical for identifying which capabilities need to be enabled or matured with technology. The No. 1 motivating factor for investing in supply chain technology for organizations is the need to make the decision-making process faster, more intelligent and of higher quality.

I also found it interesting that supporting new operating models was #2. This supports research from the Economist about the direction of travel of operating models leading to differing models, and well what I can I say about trading partner networks!

 

Art of Procurement – Orchestration

This report from Art of Procurement & Oro looks at how tasks are currently and potentially orchestrated in the future. By understanding how everything connects can procurement design a user experience that supports what the business needs.

Orchestration is enabling and empowering people and tools. It’s all about making their lives easier with the user experience.

Some interesting takeaways. 53% identified spreadsheets as  a wat to coordinate processes, and 48% said email. These two whilst useful still contribute far too much in procurement, which as a results slows down and makes at least semi manual most processes. Which is reflected in the stat that only 2.9% have completed automated procurement processes, with nearly 38% being 50/50.

With all of that, I’m not surprised that around 40% do not agree that procurement is easy to work with. (in fact I’m surprised its not higher)!

 

IDC Worldwide Procurement

One of a few reports released by IDC this month. Two notable takeaways for me, Users identified risk management (14%), the sustainment of cost savings (13%)  and spend data analytics (10%)  as the top three functions on which they spend their time. Risk management rose to the top identified function, again reflective of supply chain challenges.

I mentioned spreadsheets earlier and this report also identifies the top functions used by procurement, with analytics and spend data at 40%, spend visibility at 35% and supplier data at 31%. If procurement is to truly concentrate on adding value, it must digitise these processes to both get better insights, more time and single views ASAP.

As always reach out to discuss more, and always happy to hear your thoughts!

Labels:

Hi, 

I'm facing the same issue.

Please follow the steps of our tests:

 1. We do changes in the employee profile in EC - today 18/11; last run data 15/11

2. Import Employee History from Employee Central -> By Employee Name 

3. Error Message: "could not be imported because the user has Employee Central data that have changed since the last run of the "Employee Central History Processor" job."

From my understanding of the shared information above, the error message occurs since the alteration was performed after the last run of the EH. In order to capture this new changes in the employee history we have to run the full EH, is this correct? 

In short, from user story 3 this new release feature can only be used in case of the change was performed before the run of the EH and due to some misalignment of information the change was missed. In this case we can use the Import Employee History from Employee Central -> By Employee Name feature can be used. 

@xavierlegarrec, Appreciate if you could validate my line of thinking.

Thank you.

entry.title: The Many Paths to BI 2025
entry.content:
If there’s one thing we know from helping our customers with SAP BusinessObjects upgrades for over 20 years, it’s that no two deployments are the same. Each deployment has its particular qualities and needs.

Similarly, every upgrade is different, which is why there is not a single path to BI 2025. Where you end up – and the path you take to get there – largely depends on where you start.

Updates to the BI 2025 Timelines and Maintenance Program

Recently, SAP announced changes to the BI 2025 timelines, as well as to its maintenance program. BI 2025 now become available in March 2025. However, while the announcement does not include an extension of support for BI 4.2, very notable is the news that mainstream maintenance for BI 4.3 is now extended by a year to the end of 2026. Therefore, an immediate upgrade to BI 4.3 makes even more sense.

 

Some organizations are still asking themselves whether they should upgrade to BI 4.3. The answer is a resounding yes, because Priority One support for BI 4.2 is over at the end of 2024. No matter what role SAP BusinessObjects plays in your analytics landscape, you need to upgrade to maintain a version that will receive patches from SAP.

A Mature Choice

SAP BusinessObjects is a mature business intelligence platform, which means that its security model and semantic layer are also mature, and many organizations, large and small, value this maturity. It has been fulfilling its purpose reliably for a very long time.

If it ain’t broke, don’t fix it, right? Well no, one of the main reasons to upgrade is so that it stays fixed in the face of changing OS and DB technologies.

In addition:

  • The BI 4.3 Fiori-based user interface may seem foreign to experienced users, but learning it now will make the BI 2025 user interface feel that much more natural.
  • If you have BI 4.2 running on premise, then upgrading to BI 4.3 also offers an opportunity to move your system to the cloud.
  • BI 2025 strategy is based on 3 pillars: reach more business users, increase power users’ productivity, and reduce total cost of ownership (per Gregory Botticchio)

So upgrading is not merely running in place. BI 2025 is a streamlined user and IT experience designed to meet your strategic needs and budget.

Your Upgrade Is Different, but It Rhymes with all the Others

Upgrading SAP BusinessObjects can be like moving into a new house. You have to overcome the unfamiliarity before you can function to your expectations. Sometimes things don’t move with you to the new house, whether by design or by accident. In BI 2025, you will no longer have UNV universes, multi-source universes, SAP BusinessObjects Analysis edition for OLAP (AOLAP), Live Office, and Crystal Reports for Enterprise, so you will have to seek alternatives.

Just as important is what you choose to bring with you on your path to BI 2025.

How do you ensure that your upgraded deployment is everything you need it to be? But not more than you need it to be? A smooth move to BI 4.3 and BI 2025 requires planning, and planning requires clear information about your system.

Fortunately, while each upgrade is different, they should all follow a simple pattern in which you:

  • Establish a clear picture of the content in your SAP BusinessObjects system
  • Develop clarity about high- and low-priority content in your system
  • Store and secure your system content, and archive legacy assets
  • Streamline your system content to reduce your upgrade workload
  • Perform a structured and managed upgrade process

Metadata Is Key

To establish a clear picture of your content, you need to know not just what content exists there, but also where it is, who owns it, and what type of content it is.

In order to develop the critical clarity about which content is high priority for migration, you must have a strong understanding of content usage (when and by whom) and non-usage (since when).

Your system’s metadata will provide the information you need to see what content you have, what to prioritize, and what to remove. However, querying this metadata manually may be labor-intensive and prone to human error, so an automated solution is your best bet for developing a complete inventory in an efficient and effective manner.

APOS Insight is such an automated solution. Use it to scan your system and achieve a full understanding of content inventory, structure, security, and usage. It features pre-built audit reports so you can get started immediately, or open access to exhaustively interrogate the system metadata to understand your unique system deployment questions.

 

All Content Has Value

Yes, all content has value, but not equal value, and value changes over time. You should keep all of your content, but a substantial portion of it can be archived.

After scanning your system and creating a full inventory, you can determine which content you want to carry forward through your migration. Store and secure your system content, and archive legacy assets to streamline your system content and reduce your upgrade workload.

APOS Storage Center is a time-tested, automated solution that lets you create a complete backup of your system content and archive the content you don’t need to migrate. The difference between archiving manually and automating this task is that Storage Center will allow you to restore archived content selectively as needed. It will also allow you to develop an automated system clean-up routine to prevent content getting out of control in the future.

Managing Technical Debt

Technical debt is inevitable, but it can be managed and minimized.

Technical debt is accrued work that is “owed” to an IT system. This accrual occurs when you delay or shortcut performing regular maintenance. If you don’t manage technical debt, the system may become unstable and stop performing to expectations.

In SAP BusinessObjects, technical debt accrues when you don’t remove content that is no longer useful. It takes the form of unused report objects, report instances, universes and connections. It continues to accrue when you don’t properly manage users, groups, security and schedules.

A structured and managed upgrade process can reduce technical debt to a manageable minimum.

If you are interested in learning more, APOS is hosting a webinar on December 5, 2024, featuring SAP’s Eric Fenollosa, that takes a deeper dive into these concepts and actions. The webinar will be available on demand after that date.

entry.title: S/4HANA Analytics: From Fiori app to a CDS view
entry.content:
S/4HANA Analytics: From Fiori app to a CDS view

 

If you work with S/4HANA Embedded Analytics as I do, you might share the impression that analyzing or researching the system can sometimes feel more time-consuming than coding or configuring reports yourself. In this post, I outline how to trace your path from an Analytical Fiori App to a CDS (Core Data Services) view—and beyond.

 

Why Should You Care?

The primary reason to understand this process is the reusability of built-in content. This can be beneficial for:

  • Extracting data for BW, SAP Datasphere, or other platforms.
  • Using the same CDS view in external BI tools like Analysis for Office or SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC).

 

The Process Explained

 

Start with the Fiori Apps Library Fiori Apps Library

 

I typically work in on-premise environments, so my first step is to search within the Fiori Apps Library with filters set for S/4HANA on-premise and ‘Analytical’ application types.

Begin your search on Overview pages, but feel free to tailor your search to your needs.

 

Once you identify a relevant app, there are generally three places where you can find the source CDS view:

  • The Library documentation itself.
  • A linked Help page.
  • The App Configuration.

In the documentation itself

 

This is the most straightforward scenario. If you find a promising app, switch to the Implementation Information tab, expand Configuration, and look for OData services with the _CDS suffix. Copy the name without the _CDS suffix, and you have your view.

 

Let us take an example of a Procurement Overview Page, here we can see the 3 CDS views listed for us:

 

In the Help page linked with documentation

 

If the documentation doesn’t specify a CDS view, return to the Product Features tab and locate any linked Help page.

 

An example of Supply Demand Overview - Detailed Analysis shows us just that.

 

On the Help page, you may find a list of relevant CDS views to explore.

 

 

 

Through App Configuration

 

If neither the Fiori Apps Library nor the Help page provides clear clues, but the app in question is an Analytical WebDynpro application, there’s still hope.

 

You can either find your CDS view further down in the Configuration or by launching a Fiori App in your sandbox environment.

 

Let us look at the example of Material Inventory Values - Balance Summary – as you can see, there is no specific OData service mentioned in the documentation.

Launch the Fiori app in your sandbox environment using the URL format: _host_/sap/bc/webdynpro/sap/_WD_application_

For the Material Inventory Values - Balance Summary app, use a URL like:

host:50080/sap/bc/webdynpro/sap/fis_fpm_ovp_ml_balsum

 

If this is a Web-Dynpro-based grid (very frequently the case), look for the Query Information section.

 

 

Scroll to find the Query Technical Name (e.g., 2CCMLBALSUM). Remove the 2C prefix and search for this in ABAP Development Tools (ADT) for Eclipse.

 

Bonus Tip: BEx Queries

Sometimes, you might encounter a BEx Query in the Configuration section of the Fiori Apps Library. Don’t be deterred; this means you’re close. Remove the 2C prefix from the query name and search for the corresponding CDS view in ADT.

 

Conclusion

There is no single, direct path from an Analytical Fiori App to a CDS view. However, by following these three approaches—Library documentation, linked Help pages, and App Configuration—you can confidently find what you need. I hope these methods assist you as they have helped me.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Functionality for EDI Processing: Setup and Testing of API for Inbound Order Creation
entry.content:
From IDOCs to APIs

 

 

In my previous posts, I described various aspects of EDI inbound order processing using the classical IDOC-based approach. However, SAP considers IDOCs an outdated technology, and the use of APIs is recommended for S/4HANA implementations. The purpose of this article is to demonstrate how a functional consultant can select and test an API for sales order creation.

This article explains how to select an API and test a SOAP API using simple requests directly with system master data. The next article will cover mapping external data, such as customer partner and material numbers, GLNs, EAN/GTINs, and sales area determination.

How to select API for Sales order creation: B2B API

Let's start from selection of API for Sales order creation for SAP S/4 HANA Cloud Private Edition. The starting point for searching for APIs is the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

By selecting the APIs section and filtering by the product SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, you will find a wide range of more or less relevant results. The most important factor for selection is the purpose coded as A2A, A2X, and B2B. It’s also essential to note whether the API is marked as ODATA or SOAP, as this affects the implementation and testing technique. However, I believe the purpose is the primary criterion for selection, while the API type should be considered secondarily.

api.sap.com

A2X, A2A, or B2B - What to Select for EDI Interchange?

The SAP Help Portal provides the following explanation of these API types in the APIs for Sales article:

  • A2X stands for application to X users. You can use an A2X service to connect SAP S/4HANA with another system in your system landscape. It is an OData-based web service with a synchronous execution.
  • A2A stands for application to application. You can use an A2A service to connect SAP S/4HANA with another system in your system landscape. It is a SOAP-based web service with an asynchronous execution.
  • B2B stands for business to business. You can use a B2B service to connect SAP S/4HANA with a system in another system landscape. It is a SOAP-based web service with an asynchronous execution.

Based on this description, I conclude that for EDI purpose, B2B is the more applicable option.

Indeed, if you compare body structure of A2X and B2B, you will find the difference. B2B provides wide options for external data, such as GLNs, external partner numbers, EAN/GTINSs, Customer material info records (CMIR) and so on. Alternatively, A2X looks like a reflection of SAP standard tables and dedicated to work with internal SAP identifiers. 

B2B vs A2X: partner details

Based on the explanation above, I selected the "Sales Order/Customer Return - Create, Update, Cancel (B2B)" API.

Basic information about this API is available on the API page of SAP Business Accelerator Hub. The Documentation tab on the API page provides a link to help.sap.com , where additional details can be found, including configuration steps and sample payloads. The technical name of this service is ORDERREQUEST_IN, which will be needed later.

Setup for Testing: AIF

The SOAP API OrderRequest_In uses the SAP Application Interface Framework (AIF) for processing and monitoring. To begin, ensure that the scenario ID SAP_COM_0223 is deployed as described in the relevant section of SAP Help.

Checking Deployment

To verify if the scenario is already deployed:

  1. Use the monitoring transaction code /AIF/ERR - Monitoring and Error Handling .
    As with all transaction codes starting with /, run it as /n/AIF/ERR from the Command field or add it to your Favorites.
  2. Check if /SDEDI Sales & Distribution EDI Integration is available in the Namespace search help. If it is not available, you will need to deploy it.
    SAP "Monitoring and Error Handling" screen with '/SDEDI' namespace for Sales & Distribution EDI Integration.

Sales & Distribution EDI Integration Deployment

To deploy the required content:

  1. Use transaction code /AIF/CONTENT_EXTRACT (AIF Content Transport – Deploy).
  2. Add the deployment scenario ID SAP_COM_0223.
  3. Press F8 (Execute) to complete the deployment.

"AIF Content Transport" screen for deploying scenario ID SAP_COM_0223.

Confirmation of Successful Deployment:

Deployment Scenario SAP_COM_0223 successfully extracted.

AIF Licensing

There has been some discussion in the community about whether AIF requires additional licensing. Based on my research, AIF services provided within the SAP namespace do not require extra licensing. In this case, the service falls under the /SDEDI namespace, so no separate licensing is necessary.

However, it is recommended to confirm this with the responsible person in your organization for complete assurance.

SOAP API Testing Using SE80 Object Navigator

A common method for testing API is sending requests to the SAP system using tools like Postman. This option mimics real interchange but requires additional effort to learn new tools and address potential infrastructure challenges such as connectivity and access. For processing a payload by the SAP S/4HANA system without infrastructure issues, a simpler option is testing directly within the system using SE80 Object Navigator.

Run t-code SE80 Object Navigator. If Enterprise Service Browser is unavailable, enable it: Utilities > Settings > Workbench (General) > Enterprise Services Browser.

Enabling Enterprise Services Browser in SE80 ABAP Workbench settings under Utilities > Settings.

Please select Enterprise Services Browser and open the Web Service Definition ORDERREQUEST_IN.

SE80: Accessing ORDERREQUEST_IN in Enterprise Services Browser under Web Service Definition.

Press F8 to test.

SE80: Displaying OrderRequest_In service definition details for creating, changing, or deleting sales orders.

Notice: Another available option is to navigate through: Repository Browser > Package > LOG_EDI_INTEGRATION > Subpackages > LOG_EDI_SD > Subpackages > LOG_EDI_SD_IMPL_ORDER > Enterprise Services > Service Definitions > ORDERREQUEST_IN.

SE80: Detailed view of OrderRequest_In service definition within LOG_EDI_INTEGRATION package.

Select Input Template

  1. Request template: informative but complex
  2. Template without payload: easy to paste your payload

SE80: Test Service Provider screen for ORDER_REQUEST_IN with options to generate a request template.

Example below is a template without payload. Switch to Editor (1) to paste or edit text, or Load an existing test file (2)

SE80: Test Service Provider: Display Request screen showing XML template for OrderRequest structure.

It is also possible to save and open payloads using the Save variant and Get variant options.

SE80: Save variant and Get variant.

When Request is prepared by Editor or loaded, following features are helpful:

  1. Execute request
  2. Check XML
  3. Download the request for future use
  4. Adjust XML for better readability

SE80: XML Request editor

Run the request:  F8 Execute. A notice indicates successful processing for this API, meaning that the sales order has been created.

Successful with empty result - commit may be required.

Run VA03 to find a created Sales Order. The last order created by the user will be used as the Order number. Alternatively, use VA05 to find created Orders. More monitoring options are in the next section. The error response below indicates a request with the same PO number was previously sent.

SE80: XML Response with Error Description

Basic Payload Example

When starting testing, I prefer to use a simple example based on an existing sales order in the system. Testing with a clear and straightforward case makes debugging easier, minimizing references to EDI-specific logic.

To create such an example:

  1. Identify an Existing Sales Order: Select a sales order already present in the system.
  2. Recreate It Manually: Ensure the settings and master data are valid by manually recreating the order.
  3. Generate Payload: Use a simple template to create the payload based on this verified sales order.

Below is an example of a basic order payload. This payload contains the minimal required data to create a sales order using system data directly, without any mapping. 

  • Message Header Data: Mandatory but can use any placeholder values for basic testing.
  • Order Data: Must be populated with relevant data, such as details from an existing sales order.

Additionally, I included comments within the payload. These comments can be excluded as needed. This example is particularly useful for training purposes or for simulating intercompany scenarios where both systems involved in the interchange use the same master data.

Order Request Payload

 

 

 

 

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
 <MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2024-11-10T02:53:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
   <InternalID>DummyS</InternalID>
  </SenderParty>
 </MessageHeader>
 <Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_1</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-10-23</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <!-- Order type and Sales area-->
  <SalesDocumentType>ZOR</SalesDocumentType>
  <SalesOrganization>1000</SalesOrganization>
  <DistributionChannel>10</DistributionChannel>
  <OrganizationDivision>00</OrganizationDivision>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <!-- SAP number of Sold-to party-->
   <SupplierPartyID>211</SupplierPartyID>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <!-- SAP number of Ship-to party-->
   <SupplierPartyID>212</SupplierPartyID>
  </Party>
  <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">1.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <!-- SAP number of material-->
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
   </Product>
  </OrderItem>
 </Order>
</n0:OrderRequest>

 

 

 

 

This basic example provides a minimal setup for creating a sales order while using direct system data. Feel free to adjust the values based on your specific scenario or testing needs.

Let’s test using SE80, as explained above: prepare an payload and Press F8 Execute:

Simple XML Order Request

Result:

Processing result

VA03 Display sales order: Highlighted fields, such as those populated from the payload data, including Sold-To Party, Ship-To Party, and Order Type, are displayed. Other fields, like Payment Terms, Incoterms, and Item Description, are automatically populated based on the system's master data and configuration settings.

VA03 Sales order

It’s important to note that the master data must be sufficiently complete. For example, if Incoterms or Payment Terms are missing from the customer master record, an error may occur during processing. To address this issue, you can either update the master data to include the missing information or add the required data directly to the payload.

Extended Payload Example

This is another “simple” example that directly uses system data without relying on EDI-specific settings. However, it demonstrates several important EDI-related features:

  • OrderItemText: If provided, this field replaces the text from the material master data.
  • BuyerProductID: Even if not used for mapping, it is stored in the related item field and can be utilized for outputs.
  • ExpectedNetPrice: This value is mapped to the EDI1 price condition.
  • ExpectedNetAmount: This value is mapped to the EDI2 price condition.

 

 

 

 

<n0:OrderRequest xmlns:n0="http://sap.com/xi/EDI">
 <MessageHeader>
  <ID>Message_Header_ID</ID>
  <CreationDateTime>2020-04-10T02:53:20Z</CreationDateTime>
  <SenderParty>
   <InternalID>DummyS</InternalID>
  </SenderParty>
 </MessageHeader>
 <Order>
  <PurchaseOrderID>B2B_API_test_1.1</PurchaseOrderID>
  <PurchaseOrderCreationDate>2024-10-23</PurchaseOrderCreationDate>
  <SalesDocumentType>ZOR</SalesDocumentType>
  <SalesOrganization>1000</SalesOrganization>
  <DistributionChannel>10</DistributionChannel>
  <OrganizationDivision>00</OrganizationDivision>
  <Party PartyType="SoldTo">
   <SupplierPartyID>211</SupplierPartyID>
  </Party>
  <Party PartyType="ShipTo">
   <SupplierPartyID>212</SupplierPartyID>
  </Party>
  <OrderItem>
   <PurchaseOrderItemID>10</PurchaseOrderItemID>
   <OrderItemText>Customer Order item text</OrderItemText>
   <RequestedQuantity unitCode="PCE">10.0</RequestedQuantity>
   <Product>
    <SupplierProductID>1521</SupplierProductID>
    <BuyerProductID>CustMatID</BuyerProductID>
   </Product>
   <ExpectedNetPrice>
    <Amount currencyCode="NOK">11</Amount>
    <BaseQuantity unitCode="PCE">1</BaseQuantity>
   </ExpectedNetPrice>
   <ExpectedNetAmount currencyCode="NOK">110</ExpectedNetAmount>
  </OrderItem>
 </Order>
</n0:OrderRequest>

 

 

 

Sales order:

VA03 Sales Order: customer material information

VA03: Sales order, Customer expected Price and Value

Monitoring of SOAP API Processing

Since SOAP API processing is based on the Application Interface Framework (AIF), AIF-related tools can be used for monitoring and error handling.

/AIF/ERR - Monitoring and Error Handling. To improve performance and reduce the volume of data displayed, apply the following filters on the selection screen:

  • Application: AIF
  • Namespace: /SDEDI

Additionally, adjust the status selection to include both error and successful messages to gain a comprehensive view of the processing result

/AIF/ERR - Monitoring and Error Handling: Selection screen

When you run the report, messages are displayed in a structured format, organized by Sender ID (from the message header) and PO number.

  • To view details, select a message from the list.
  • The log records will display information about either successful processing or error occurrences, providing clarity on the message status and any required actions.

/AIF/ERR - Monitoring and Error Handling: ALV screen

/AIF/LOG - Interface Logs. Another available option for monitoring. To narrow down the results and generate a focused report, select a relevant Subobject during the selection process.

/AIF/LOG - Interface Logs: Selection screen.

Select message on the upper screen and find the log records on the lower screen.

/AIF/LOG - Interface Logs: ALV screen

The transactions SRT_MONI (WS Message Monitor) and SRT_UTIL (Web Service Utilities) are frequently mentioned in the context of SOAP monitoring. However they are dedicated for lower transmission infrastructure level and not relevant for payload processing errors and monitoring of messages created by SE80 test technique.

Handling Errors

Successfully processing an inbound message on the first attempt is very rare. Typically, it requires an iterative process of running the message, adjusting the payload, and refining the system setup or master data.

SOAP API processing provides informative error messages as part of the response to help you identify and resolve issues. Below are some common errors and their possible causes.

Typical Errors and Their Causes

Class II_EDI_ORDER_REQUEST_IN is not a proxy class. This error appears if AIF deployment scenario hasn’t been extracted.

Error response: Class II_EDI_ORDER_REQUEST_IN is not a proxy class.

The Source Party internal ID must be provided in the source data. Message header sender data is not complete.

Error response: The Source Party internal ID must be provided in the source data.

Sales area cannot be determined. This occurs when key parameters are missing: Sales Order Type, Sales Organization, Distribution Channel, or Division. Ensure these are provided or configured for determination by external data.

Error response: Sales area cannot be determined.

Supplier Party ID 0000001111 is not found. This occurs when the provided Supplier Party ID does not match the system's master data. Ensure the ID is correct and exists in the system.

Error response: Supplier Party ID 0000001111 is not found.

Business partner for role Sold-to party cannot be determined. This occurs when Supplier party ID is missing, and Buyer party ID isn’t configured for external determination.

Error response: Business partner for role Sold-to party cannot be determined.

Material 1521-00 is not defined for sales org.1000, distr.chan.10, language EN. This occurs when the provided Supplier Product ID does not match the system's master data.

Error response: Material 1521-00 is not defined for sales org. 1000, distr. chan. 10, language EN.

Acknowledgements and System Overview

I would like to thank my colleagues, Askar Timofeev and Vadim Vologzhanin, for helping me learn this topic. I also extend my gratitude to Capgemini for providing a sandbox system, which I used for investigations, creating test examples, and preparing screenshots. The sandbox was SAP S/4HANA 2022 (S/4HANA On-Premise Release 2022 SP 01, February 2023 FPS).

entry.title: SAP HCM for SAP Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I have spent many days exploring documentation on RISE with SAP and the conversion guides for transitioning for SAP ERP to SAP Hana Cloud Private Edition. Globally I do have an overview of how this is working and what are the steps to be taken for the conversion process. 

The scenario I do not understand regarding the conversion process is when SAP ERP system is used for different modules like FI, CO, SD, MM and also HCM within the same client and the business need is to only migrate HCM to SAP Cloud Private Edition  and keep the rest of the modules (FI, SD, MM ) as they are on the SAP ERP system. Is it possible to only migrate HCM to SAP Cloud Private Edition or the whole SAP ERP system should be converted including all modules?  during the conversion process is there any step where only specific modules and application can be selected for the conversion?

Many thanks

Rachid.

entry.title: Crew Management in SAP Field Service Management
entry.content:
Understanding Crew Management in SAP FSM

Crew management in SAP FSM involves the strategic organization and deployment of service teams to carry out field tasks. Crews are essential in any field service for several key reasons, including efficiency, expertise, safety, scalability, and training and development. 

Here’s a detailed look at why crews are necessary in field service management:

1. Efficiency and Productivity:

  • Task Division: Crews allow for the division of labor, enabling team members to specialize in different tasks. This specialization improves efficiency, as each team member can focus on what they do best.
  • Reduced downtime: With a team, multiple tasks can be handled simultaneously. For example, in a utility repair situation, while one member secures the area, another can diagnose the issue, and yet another can begin repairs.

2. Expertise and Skill Diversity:

  • Varied Expertise: Field service tasks often require a range of skills and knowledge. A crew combines individuals with different areas of expertise, ensuring that complex jobs can be handled effectively.
  • Problem Solving: Diverse skill sets within a crew foster collaborative problem-solving, leading to better and faster resolutions of service issues.

3. Safety:

  • Compliance with Safety Standards: Many field service jobs, such as those in utilities or construction, involve dangerous tasks. A crew ensures that safety protocols are followed, and team members can look out for each other.
  • Handling Emergencies: In case of an emergency, having a crew on site ensures that immediate assistance is available. This is crucial to preventing accidents and managing risks effectively.

4. Scalability and Flexibility:

  • Handling Large Projects: Some field service tasks are too large or complex for a single individual. Crews make it possible to scale up operations to handle these tasks efficiently.
  • Adaptability: Crews can be scaled up or down based on the size and complexity of the job, providing flexibility in resource allocation.

5. Training and Development:

  • On-the-Job Training: Newer or less experienced crew members can learn from more seasoned professionals, gaining practical knowledge and skills in real-world situations.
  • Knowledge Sharing: Crews facilitate knowledge transfer and the sharing of best practices, contributing to overall team competence and improvement.

SAP FSM integrates these functions into a cohesive system that facilitates seamless coordination between dispatchers, technicians, and crew leaders. With Cew Management, planners can effectively manage teams to meet a field service schedule that often changes. They can create crews, search for and add crew leads and members with the necessary skills, adjust membership dates to match appointment times, and monitor all crew schedules and members in one place.

Getting Started with Crews 

To create your first crew, navigate to the crew menu from the side menu of the SAP FSM application:

  1. Go to Master Data.
  2. Select Crews.
  3. Click on Create Crew.

Fig 1: Create a Crew

Fig 2: Create Crew when few already exists in system 

Provide a name for the crew, a description, the project for which the crew will be working, the minimum crew size, and the capacity in hours per day.

Fig 3: Quick Create Crew

 

Searching Crews

Use the search bar to find a specific crew and even save it for later use.

Fig 3: Search Crews

Filter Crews

Apply filters and save filter criteria for future use. 

Fig 4: Filter Crew

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Activate/De-activate Crews

 Use the three-dot menu in the upper-right corner of the header. De-activating crews will ensure that particular crew is no longer available for planning.

Fig 5: Activate/De-activate Crew

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

General Information: Maintain details such as crew name, description, minimum crew size, capacity, optional project, region, and time zone. Ensure you’re in Edit mode to make changes.

Fig 6: General Information in Crew Management

 

Required Skills: Add or remove skills necessary for the crew, and search for specific skills. You must be in Edit mode to enable the Add button.

Fig 7: Required Skills in Crew Management

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Assigned People: View the crew constellation in a Kanban view, adjustable by date range. For smaller screens, switch to full-screen mode for better visibility.

Fig 7: Assigned People in Crew

 

Using Kanban and List Views

Kanban View:

  • Displays crew constellations based on the selected date range.
  • Navigate using the “<” and “>” arrows to move forward or backward in two-week intervals.
  • The default date range is set to two weeks from today to optimize loading times.

Fig 9: Kanban view of the Crew.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

List View:

  • Ideal for managing fast-changing and less permanent crew constellations.
  • Switch between Kanban and list views by clicking the grid icon.
  • Use standard search, filter, and group functionalities to manage crew assignments.
  • Save filter criteria and group crew assignments by person’s name for easy sorting.

Fig 9: List view of Crew Constellation

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

By leveraging these features in SAP FSM, you can streamline crew management, ensuring that the right teams are in place to deliver exceptional field service.

 

Adding a Crew Lead in SAP Field Service Management

After creating a new crew in SAP Field Service Management (SAP FSM), the next step is to designate a crew lead. This can be done using either the Kanban view or the List view. Note that only one person can be selected as the crew lead.

Option 1: Adding a Crew Lead in Kanban View

  • Enter Edit Mode: Click on the Edit button
  • Add Crew Lead: Choose Add > Crew Lead.
  • Select Person: In the Add Crew Lead dialog, select or search for the person you want to designate as the crew lead.
    • Assignment Type: Choose between Permanent or Limited assignment
    • Permanent Assignment: Allows you to choose only the start date. The end date is fixed
    • Limited Assignment: Allows you to set both start and end dates.
  • Enter Dates: Input the assignment dates.
  • Save: Click Add, and then Save.

Option 2: Adding a Crew Lead in List View

  • Enter Edit Mode: Click on the Edit button.
  • Add via the Plus Button: Click the plus button (+) on a crew card for a specific day.
  • Select Person: In the Add Crew Lead dialog, select or search for the person.
    • The assignment type defaults to Limited with dates matching those on the card, but you can adjust these dates.
  • Save: Click Add, and then Save.

Note: If you add only crew members and leave the lead section empty, the system will automatically designate one of the members as the lead. The crew lead is set as the responsible person and will be visible in the mobile app.

By following these steps, you can ensure that each crew has a designated leader, improving coordination and efficiency in your field service operations.

Changing the Crew Lead in SAP Field Service Management

In SAP Field Service Management (SAP FSM), a crew must always have a designated leader. While you cannot remove a crew leader without first appointing a new one, you can replace the leader with an existing crew member. Here’s how to do it:

Steps to Change the Crew Lead

  • Enter Edit Mode: Click on the Edit button.
  • Select New Leader: Select the crew member you want to promote to crew lead.
  • Promote to Crew Lead:
    • Right-click on the selected member.
    • Choose Make Crew Lead from the context menu.
  • Remove Previous Leader (Optional):
    • Right-click on the previous crew lead.
    • Choose Remove from Crew if you need to remove them entirely from the crew.
  • Save Changes: Click Save to confirm the changes.

Fig 10: Replace the crew leader

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 


Note: You can only promote a member to crew lead for a particular day, not for a date range.

By following these steps, you ensure that the crew always has a designated leader, maintaining structure and responsibility within the team.

Coming up in my next blog: How to handle crew in Planning Board—stay tuned

entry.title: Re: Salesforce integration: common requirements and use cases.
entry.content:
Thanks for this thorough breakdown of integration requirements and use cases with SAP CPI. From my experience, SAP Salesforce integration benefits greatly from real-time data flows using Platform Events and Streaming API, especially for actions like opportunity creation and account updates. For example, using a trigger in Salesforce to publish platform events is an efficient approach.

I also find Advantco’s Workbench to be a powerful tool. It simplifies schema generation, payload testing, and query building (refer to WB2-Schema-generation-1, WB3-Test-payloads, and WB4-Query-builder-1 images) without the usual hurdles of permissions or version management. Additionally, the ability to test APEX REST calls directly (as shown in WB5-Test-REST-APEX-calls) adds flexibility, especially for unique business cases where standard APIs don’t meet requirements.

For batch processing, the Advantco adapter’s automation of BULK API operations is a major time-saver, allowing seamless switching between REST and BULK APIs. These features help streamline development and maintenance, ensuring efficient handling of both small and large data volumes.

I’d be interested to hear more about others' experiences with these tools, especially around pagination handling and optimizing batch loads.

entry.title: Training Needs Analysis in LMS
entry.content:
Our client has a process called training needs analysis where they will launch a survey for training needs analysis of employee, basically from this process they will get the recommendations from employees that what types of training and what are the trainings they will expecting from the organization in future.

Current process of client without LMS:  they will launch a survey in the email >>> the employee will fill and send back the email >>> then the L&D person will create a file manually based on the survey filled by the employees >>> the manually file will be converted as a report also, they will showcase that this employee xxx needs xxx training in future.

Expectation from LMS: the above process should be implemented into the system >>> the only survey should be launched without linking with item >>> then based on survey filled by the employees there should be fi- le created in the automatically called (Training needs analysis) where the file shows the recommended trainings filled by the employees in the survey >>> then for more clarification the report should be used

NOTE: My client is asking in SAP SuccessFactors we not have a Training needs analysis ?
